exemplar.title: An investigation into schema theory and its impacts on gender perceptions

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation into schema theory and its impacts on gender perceptions

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim clearly states purpose and explains contemporary relevance
Schema theory is accurately described and explicitly linked to the investigation
Independent and dependent variables are correctly stated and operationalized
weaknesses: Minor stylistic lapses (e.g., phrasing âmedianâ in hypothesis) prevent perfection
Theoretical depth is solid but not exhaustive
Hypothesis phrasing could more clearly reference raw ratings rather than statistical measures
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three introduction strands consistently achieved topâband performance (band 5) with clear explanation of aim, theory linkage, and operationalized variables. Minor phrasing and depth issues justify a 5 rather than 6.

strengths: Research design is explained with clear rationale to avoid demand and order effects
Sampling technique is justified in terms of feasibility and limitations
Controlled variables and materials are listed with reasons for each control
weaknesses: Imbalance in stimulus materials (17 male vs. 19 female paragraphs) may introduce bias
Sample size distribution by group is not fully detailed
Procedure numbering issues and some redundant descriptions reduce clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each exploration strand meets the top band (3â4) with explanations of design, sampling, and controls. The depth and justification warrant the maximum mark of 4 for this criterion.

strengths: Appropriate descriptive and inferential statistics (median, IQR, MannâWhitney U) applied accurately
Graphical presentation correctly addresses the hypothesis with clear labels
Findings are interpreted in relation to data and hypothesis, with correct decision on null hypothesis
weaknesses: Minor formatting and reporting slips (dual âon averageâ, excessive pâvalue precision)
Graph caption is fragmented across sections
Some early conclusions drawn before inferential results could be more tightly sequenced
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical and graphical analysis strands are solidly in the top band with accurate application and clear interpretation. Minor technical and formatting issues prevent the perfect 6.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are explained and directly linked to validity and bias concerns
Modifications are explicitly tied to identified limitations and fully justified
Findings are discussed with reference to schema theory and compared to prior study
weaknesses: Findings discussion, while accurate, lacks deeper theoretical elaboration
Some overstatements about sampling balance given actual gender ratio
Implications for future practice could be expanded
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands demonstrate thorough insight, clear linkage of modifications to limitations, and reference to theory, meriting topâband performance (5â6). The discussionâs relative brevity and slight overstatements preclude a 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A box-and-whisker plot is the correct technique for medians/IQRs and directly addresses the hypothesis. Axes, labels and sample sizes are present and appropriate. Very slight resolution issues in the image keep it from full marks, but criteria for accurate presentation are met, meriting 5.

The student clearly interprets statistical output, explicitly links the non-significant result to accepting the null hypothesis, and references the descriptive data (medians) to explain direction. Interpretation is accurate and firmly tied to the hypothesis, satisfying top-band expectations (score 5).

Both IV (candidate gender) and DV (rating on 1â10 scale) are stated and operationalised. Directional (one-tailed) and null hypotheses are written correctly. Minor phrasing issues (use of âmedianâ inside hypothesis) prevent a 6, but the criteria for the 5-6 band are clearly met.

Convenience sampling is not only described but justified in terms of feasibility and time constraints; participant demographics, numbers and rationale for excluding psychology students are provided. The explanation of why this technique may limit representativeness shows understanding, fulfilling band 3-4, mid-band score chosen.

Schema theory is outlined (schemas, stereotypes, influence on information processing) and its connection to the investigation is explicitly drawn (gender stereotypes as a form of schema shaping hiring perceptions). The description is accurate and the link is made explicit; however, theoretical depth is moderate rather than exhaustive, so 5/6 is appropriate.

Findings are described in relation to schema theory and directly compared with the original Fidell study, noting temporal/contextual factors. Discussion shows understanding but remains relatively brief and lacks deeper theoretical elaboration, fitting comfortably in the 3-4 band at the upper end (4).

The student clearly states the purpose â to test whether gender still biases perceptions of candidate value in a modern adolescent sample â and explicitly explains the contemporary relevance (implications for the future employment practices of todayâs students). The explanation is concise yet explicit, satisfying the descriptor for the top band. Minor stylistic lapses keep it from a perfect 6.

Multiple strengths and limitations for design, sampling and procedure are identified and each is explained with explicit relevance to potential bias or validity, demonstrating insight. The coverage is thorough though not exhaustive, warranting a solid 5 in the 5-6 band.

Controlled variables (setting, time of day, standardized instructions, researcher distance) are listed with brief rationales; materials are itemised and their purposes implicit. The student explains why controls were used (reduce distractions, conformity, social desirability). This meets the âexplainedâ requirement, though discussion is succinct, so 3/4.

For every key limitation the student proposes a concrete, justified modification (e.g., switch to repeated measures to reduce participant variability; use random sampling; written rather than read instructions). Each suggestion is clearly linked back to the identified weakness, meeting the descriptor for âexplicitly linked and fully justifiedâ (band 5-6, score 5).

The student explains that an independent-measures design was chosen to avoid demand characteristics and order effects, demonstrating understanding of the designâs logic. Limitations of the design are later acknowledged. This explanatory detail satisfies the 3-4 band; depth is adequate rather than exemplary, warranting 3 rather than 4.

Appropriate descriptive (median, SIQR) and inferential (one-tailed Mann-Whitney U) statistics are selected for ordinal independent-groups data; calculations, U, Z, p and decision are correct and reported clearly. Minor formatting slips (dual mention of âon averageâ) limit perfection; nonetheless accuracy satisfies 5-6 band at 5.

annotations.comment: The title succinctly conveys the investigationâs focus on schema theory and gender perceptions, providing clear scope and context.
The summary of Fidellâs (1970) methodology and findings is clear and thorough, effectively setting the historical context for the replication.
The schema theory description would benefit from an explicit statement linking specific cognitive processes (e.g., schema activation) to the rating task used in this investigation.
The aim articulates both the investigative purpose and contemporary relevance, making clear why findings matter for future recruitment practices.
The adaptation of candidate paragraphs is described, but the rationale for each content change (grades vs. work experience) should be elaborated to ensure validity.
The independent variable is clearly defined as candidate gender, with appropriate operationalization through paragraph versions.
The DV description focuses on the median rather than the raw rating scale; clarify the DV as the 1â10 rating first and then specify median as the chosen statistical measure.
Including âmedianâ in the hypothesis is too specific; hypotheses should refer to ratings generally. Use participantsâ ratings rather than statistical measures in Hâ.
The independent-measures design is well justified to limit demand characteristics and order effects, demonstrating solid understanding of research design trade-offs.
The sampling method description ends abruptly; it should include sample size and precise recruitment numbers to clarify representativeness.
Participant demographics are described, but the distribution across conditions (e.g., male vs. female paragraph groups) should be detailed to ensure balance.
Controlled variables are listed with rationale, but consider monitoring attention levels quantitatively to confirm that time-of-day control was effective.
The materials list shows 17 male and 19 female paragraphs, introducing imbalance. Ensure equal numbers of stimulus materials to avoid systematic bias.
The procedure repeats sampling details already described; consolidate procedural steps with sampling section to improve clarity and avoid redundancy.
Procedure numbering skips step 5 and jumps to step 6, causing confusion. Renumber sequentially for consistency.
Conclusions of sex discrimination are drawn from descriptive stats alone; interpretation should wait for inferential results before asserting discrimination.
Table 1 accurately presents median, IQR, and SIQR for both conditions, facilitating clear comparison.
The graph caption is split across two sections, reducing coherence. Merge caption lines into a single descriptive statement.
Caption continuation remains fragmented; combine with preceding caption for clarity and complete description.
The box-and-whisker plot correctly visualizes medians and IQRs, but should include sample sizes (n) on axes to contextualize variability.
Selection of a Mann-Whitney U test for nonparametric ordinal data is appropriate and demonstrates sound statistical reasoning.
The p-value is reported to five decimal places; conform to APA style by limiting to three significant digits (e.g., p = .079).
Comparing replication results to Fidell (1970) strengthens evaluation; this connection to prior findings is well articulated.
Linking non-significance to changing gender schemas in the 21st century is insightful; bolster this discussion with citations of recent studies on shifting gender biases.
The discussion of independent-measures design strengths and weaknesses is balanced and clearly linked to extraneous variable control.
Claiming gender balance in convenience sampling is overstated given the 20:16 female:male ratio; revise to reflect actual sample distribution.
The suggestion of random sampling as a modification directly addresses representativeness concerns; this modification is justified and relevant.
Identification of potential researcher effects on instruction delivery is insightful; highlighting this procedural limitation adds depth to evaluation.
The conclusion restates the accepted null hypothesis effectively, but should extend implications for future hiring practices based on findings.
The Fidell (1970) citation is incomplete in APA format and the Kognity source lacks publication details; revise for full bibliographic accuracy.
